data.title: CACI International Rightly Disputes Its Industry Classification: Oddly, That's Bullish
data.description:
William Shakespeare was wrong — or at least he didn’t always age well.

In Act II, Scene II of his classic Romeo & Juliet, the Bard has Juliet say:

What’s in a name? That which we call a rose by any other name would smell as sweet.

Try telling that to CACI International (CACI) CEO John Mengucci. He’s peeved about CACI’s industry classification.

I can’t be sure, but I suspect he may be the first CEO ever to have made an issue of this on an earnings call, the 1/22/26 call to be exact:

We have a NAICS, GICS, whatever code you want to call it, that makes us government services company.

(He also discussed his concern, about GICS specifically, at a 3/17/26 conference.)

GICS stands for Global Industry Classification Standard. NAICS stands for North American Industry ClassificationSystem.

GICS, NAICS, and others offer competing protocols for assigning companies to business classifications.

I’m most familiar with GICS. I’ve lived with it for many years at companies for which I worked in the past.

You live with it too since it’s what Seeking Alpha licenses. (To be precise, Seeking Alpha says it uses GICS for “some sector and industry” labels. No matter —Seeking Alpha lives in the world of GICS.)

Based on GICS, Seeking Alpha assigns CACI to the Industrials sector. In a GICS variation, Seeking Alpha puts CACI in an Industry called “Research and Consulting Services.” Strictly speaking this is a GICS sub-industry that’s part of the “Professional Services” industry. For what it’s worth, I think Seeking Alpha made a good choice here in how it applied GICS.

The bigger issue, the one flagged by Mengucci, is whether CACI really belongs in Professional Services or Research and Consulting Services. Based on how CACI’s business has evolved since 2012, and based on the companies cited by Mengucci as peers, I suspect he would rather CACI be categorized under Aerospace & Defense.

I agree with Mengucci.

And contrary to what Shakespeare’s Juliet might say, a Research and Consulting Services stock does not smell as sweet as one classified as Aerospace and Defense.

I say “smell” metaphorically here. But there are investment-oriented numbers that directly play to our financial senses.

Clearly, Aerospace and Defense smells much sweeter to Mr. Market than Research and Consulting Services. (The ratios are higher, but that's because the market expects better things.)

It’s thus intriguing to envision prospects for CACI stock should investors come to see it among the former.

To be fair, GICS isn’t hallucinating. At one time, the company was definitely a Research and Consulting Services company. According to NMS Consulting, this is a business…

in which external advisers help organizations think through difficult questions, design options, and carry out agreed actions. Instead of selling a product or running a standard process, consultants work with management teams on specific topics that need focused attention.

That’s a worthwhile endeavor. CACI calls it “Expertise.”

But CACI has spent more than a decade moving away from it. Today, a business it calls “Technology” contributes about 60% of revenues. And that portion is still rising.

But even this label short changes the sophistication of what CACI is doing and where it’s going.

I have to confess CACI isn’t the easiest company to grasp. So I want to start by…

Banishing Clutter

CACI is involved in a dizzying variety of defense-related programs. And it’s easy for one’s head to spin while trying to keep track of them all. Adding to the burden is the challenge a non-defense expert would face trying to get through an acronym-filled conference- or earning-call transcript without many detours to Google.

I suggest that one refrain from trying to do this. I propose focusing on the forest, not the trees.

Resist, too, the temptation to bog down in Congressional (defense) budget headlines. When high priority projects await, money finds ways to flow through. It can be through regularly budgeted money. It can be through reconciliation funding. (This is a special fast-track approach that is not subject to filibuster.)

And even when overall defense spending is reduced, high priority programs may still get funded in full or nearly in full.

CACI focuses heavily on high priority projects. As a result, it saw very modest by defense-spending standards impacts from DOGE cutbacks and relatively modest (albeit non-zero) impacts from periods of U.S. government shutdowns.

Finally, Mengucci cautioned analysts that CACI is…

not a quarter-to-quarter company. We're a year-to-year and we're going to be a decade-to-decade company.

How CACI Operates

Agile software is a phrase one often encounters in connection with CACI. This refers to a software development process where…

[i]nstead of delivering a finished product all at once (as is conventionally done), Agile breaks work into smaller, manageable cycles called sprints, each producing a functional piece of value that is delivered incrementally. Teams collaborate closely, review progress regularly, and quickly adapt based on feedback, ensuring the final product closely aligns with user expectations.

A typical sprint lasts between 2-4 weeks, allowing for rapid delivery, continuous testing, and frequent course correction. This approach reduces risk, boosts transparency, and encourages stakeholders to guide the product’s direction throughout the project.

At a 3/17/26 Bank of America conference, Mengucci discussed CACI’s version of this:

We have 1,400 people that are co-located with the 5 large combatant commanders all around the globe. That is -- that allows us to have unique access to every minute of every conflict that the U.S. is involved in to understand what technologies are working, which ones aren't, where are the gaps, how are we prosecuting different conflicts, what's the EW (Electronic Warfare) picture look like there?

That's all firsthand knowledge that our 1,400 people…. [W]e get to see that on an hourly basis. That expertise and positions like those folks, whether it's intel analysts, targeting analysts and all provide that constant feedback loop to us. We then invest ahead of customer need using our own balance sheet, not reimbursable through the U.S. government, which then allows us to sell the technology that we create in a commercial-like manner, which is exactly what this administration wants, invest ahead of customer need. We don't place very risky bets because every bet we place on building the tech is well informed by the experts that we have out in the field.

So, unlike conventional consultants that solve problems and then move on to other things, CACI’s tech specialists are embedded full-time with combat commands. And unlike conventional software companies, CACI doesn’t build something to licensee to many customers. It builds something in close collaboration with one user for that user.

No wonder classifying CACI under GICS seems like trying to shove a round peg into a square hole!

What CACI Does

The company organizes its business around seven areas. (These aren’t segments for financial reporting purposes. These are task-oriented groupings.)

  1. C3I — Command, Control, Communications, and Intelligence technology and networks.
  2. Cyber — Addressing cyber threats.
  3. Digital Solutions — Agile-at-scale process automation.
  4. Enterprise IT — Modernizing and sustaining IT ecosystems.
  5. Mission and Engineering Support — Services that help customers achieve a decisive tactical edge.
  6. Space – Helping to “deliver information to and from space along with the protection of these vital space assets using both offensive and defensive capabilities.”
  7. Spectrum Superiority – “[D]ifferentiated technology across the radio-frequency (RF) spectrum for intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance (ISR), and electronic warfare (EW).”

(Source of information and quoted text: Pages 4-5 of latest 10K)

That’s a nice list.

I particularly want right now to call out the sixth item, “Space.”

CACI has long been a very acquisitive company. But it just big-time upped the ante. On 3/9/25, CACI completed its all-cash $2.6 billion purchase of ARKA Group. The latter…

provides industry-leading electro-optical/infrared (EO/IR) and hyperspectral imaging capabilities, and Agentic AI-based software, that deliver robust geospatial intelligence for critical national security missions.

Given what we’re already seeing with the evolution of drones, missiles, tracking and targeting, it doesn’t take much imagination to see how important ARKA and space are likely to become to CACI.

No doubt ARKA is expensive. It’ll change the company’s balance sheet. (I’ll address that further below.)

As to the income statement, here’s what CFO Jeffrey MacLauchlan told attendees at the 2/18/26 Citi Global Industrial Tech and Mobility Conference:

ARKA will be accretive to our growth rate and our EBITDA margins immediately. We'll be earnings per share neutral in the first full year and accretive in the second. Those statistics that I just gave you don't presume any synergies, either revenue or cost, and we expect to realize some of both.

A Closer Look at Drones

Consider today’s hottest headlines — wars in Ukraine and now Iran (and the Middle East).

The latter isn’t about armies fighting on the ground, or even air forces dropping bombs. Missiles and drones are looming large.

Ukraine experienced a lot of conventional warfare. But lately, drones have been gaining prominence.

Consider too potential future trends in warfare. Ukraine and Iran/the Middle East may be templates for the further evolution of warfare.

Against this backdrop, consider Mengucci’s 3/17/26 remarks regarding CACI and anti-drone activity.

So in the area of drones and counter drones, a lot of field-based experience as to how our drones being flown, whether it's large near peer countries or nation states. And the lesson that those enemies change their tactics every 4 to 8 hours continuously over a 3- or 4-year period. Every 4 or 8 hours, the signature that those threats fly in is completely different than it was 4 hours earlier. So for us to understand the nature, when we say we build counter drone solutions, hundreds of systems, thousands of sensors that are out there with hundreds of realtime confirmed kills in a non-kinetic, low, no collateral manner, that's a calling card that just about every command or everybody in the United States military knows.

And I know that we're in a very busy time where a lot of companies are claiming they have counter UAS solutions. I'm willing to say we probably have some of the best because we have those embedded folks there.

(Emphasis supplied. UAS means Unmanned Aircraft Systems.)

Mengucci later said:

So how far out can you detect that threat that give you time to take the right course of action. We all know the economic side of counter-counter- drone work is we, as a nation, other partner nations cannot spend -- cannot afford to spend $2 million on a missile, take a $30,000 drone down. We've already heard that. If you let that continue, you lose the economic war. Eventually, you lose the overall war.

I have to interrupt Mengucci’s verbal flow here.

Spending $2 million to counter a $300,000 drone! That should give you a sense of how incredibly hard this kind of warfare is.

CACI, addressing, as it does problems like this, isn’t about military brawn (tanks, bombers, fighter jets, bombs, submarines, battle ships, etc.). It’s about military brains.

Remember this later as we contemplate CACI’s stock valuation!

Let’s continue as Mengucci moves into, not just brains, but the need for agility.

[O]ur solutions are at a distance that give commanders 18 minutes of decision time, not 6 seconds. Majority of what you all hear about today is 1 to 3 kilometers on a small Class 1 drone that gives a commander about 6 seconds of decision time, and you're only seeing 1 to 3 kilometers. So it takes you 2 seconds for your mind to react. You got 4 seconds to pick the right button to hit. If you miss it, you're going to going to get hit. So it's great to talk about low-cost drone solutions. There are low-cost drones, but the way to find them in either cellular or satellite-based or using radar, using passive radar, active radar, using RF, there's a multiple number of sensors you need to be able to track these things. Not only track them, but find them and then come up with targeting, you can either pass to a kinetic kill or you can non kinetically take those down.

And let’s not forget today’s favorite topic — AI. Mengucci touched on that too.

So in a software baseline that learns continuously, it's all AI based. So as we find other threats and other cyber bullets that we can send to those drones, we can update everybody's systems concurrently all over the world. So it's a very different scale, a very different level of discrimination and something that is selling very well today and will continue to sell very well.

CACI is about much more than countering drones. Remember the seven-part list presented above.

I featured this today, first because its a timely topic. Also, it vividly illustrates CACI’s special calling card — the kinds of sophisticated things it can do because of the way it embeds its people within military command operations.

The Numbers

When looking at CACI’s numbers, bear in mind that the company’s latest reporting period, ended 12/31/25, predates the very large ARKA acquisition. So don’t try to extrapolate.

But what we see will give us a sense of how CAACI generally performs, bearing in mind that acquisitions are business as usual here.

As to the relative comparisons I usually show, you got a preview above when I showed valuation comparisons.

I’m comparing CACI to medians among companies in two industries — the Research and Consulting Services industry to which GICS assigns it, and the Aerospace and Defense industry, which arguably seems more suitable. I also compare CACI to the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.

Here are the historic and forward growth rates.

Clearly, CACI fares better than the SPY Group. It’s also better than Research and Consulting Services.

It’s not fully up to speed versus Aerospace and Defense. But as operations labeled Technology by CACI comprise an escalating portion of the business mix, it could gain ground.

Here are the core fundamentals.

This table is likely to change dramatically due to the ARKA acquisition.

That’s especially so in the top four rows which relate to debt.

Returns on Equity and Assets might change a lot in the short term as the capital base quickly grows. But the profit impact should eventually restore the comparisons.

Risk

Operationally, CACI is a veteran. So this kind of risk is no more than average for CACI.

That said, there is likely to be some sentiment risk as headlines relating to defense spending and budgetary battles ebb and flow. Investors may periodically lose track of this company’s long-term story as discussed above.

That shouldn’t happen. But as experienced investors know, there can be short-term differences between how the market should be and how it actually is.

Now, considering the size of the ARKA acquisition, the Financial Statements Highlights table becomes especially interesting.

The Acquisitions line confirms that such activity has long been an important aspect of CACI.

Note, though, that the debt ratio never got out of control, at least as of the time of period-ending reports.

With respect to questions about the sharply increased leverage CACI will take on through the CACI purchase, management responded by pointing out that the company has a track record of bringing such leverage spikes back down to reasonable levels.

This suggests management will do it again following the CACI purchase.

Notice, too, that as acquisitive as CACI has been, it has been a consistent buyer of its own stock. It expects to continue doing so in the future.

Based on CACI’s track record, and my belief that ARKA will turn out to be an excellent addition to CACI, I believe financial risk here to be manageable.

What to do About CACI Stock

I showed you the valuation ratios above. But for your convenience, I’ll show them again here.

Firstly, I think CACI’s Proj. 3-5Y EPS Gr is too low. As the Street becomes more attuned to CACI’s evolution beyond consulting, I expect the projection will move up and approach that which we see for Aerospace and Defense.

Management is working very hard to make this happen by getting its story out into the investment world.

Many companies attend firm-specific conferences. But CACI has hit the road big time. It attended five since it reported December quarter results on 1/22/26. It also attended a conference shortly before the end of that quarter.

So far, the stock hasn’t been reacting.

But that could well change as further results come in after ARKA becomes integrated, and as the Street comes to further appreciate the actual relationship between GICS (consulting — yawn) and reality (the brains behind military power), not to mention global tensions and increasing need for what CACI brings to the table.

Here’s the price chart.

It’s looked good for much of the last 12 months. But the stock seems to be taking a momentum breather right now.

Notice that the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) and the 50-day EMA both look lackluster.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

CO seems to have cooled a bit, as have the moving averages.

The cooling off may reflect a wait-and-see stance regarding the just-completed ARKA purchase. That’s OK. I expect the passage of time will fix that.

But notice the CMF. It’s been noticeably more bullish. Remember, this is an institutions-oriented indicator. Perhaps all the people management has been speaking to lately are listening to, and starting to embrace CACI’s message.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see CACI as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market as ARKA contributes and as Mr. Market comes to increasingly understand the kind of company CACI truly is.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating CACI as a “Buy.”

data.title: FTXN: Solid Long-Term Choice, But Risky To Buy For Now
data.description:
The First Trust Nasdaq Oil & Gas ETF (FTXN), launched on 09/20/2016 and managed by First Trust Advisors LP, provides exposure to the oil & gas industry within the U.S. equity market. It has $157 million in AUM and charges an expense ratio of 0.6%.

FTXN may have recently caught the attention of many investors due to its superior performance relative to the market in the last couple of weeks. However, this performance is so tied to the recent oil supply shock that it hints more at a risk than an opportunity. I view this ETF as a good long-term vehicle for such targeted exposure to the industry because of its quality bias, but the current conditions do not warrant an entry at this point.

Methodology

FTXN tracks the Nasdaq US Smart Oil & Gas Index, which uses a modified factor-weighted approach. More specifically, it starts with selecting oil & gas stocks from the Nasdaq US Benchmark based on four factors: gross income, ROA, momentum, and cash flow. For momentum, it uses the average of 3-, 6-, 9-, and 12-month price returns. This way, it identifies 30 to 50 stocks that exhibit the strongest overall factor rankings.

It weights the stocks based on cash flow size in absolute dollar terms, rather than market cap, adding one more layer of quality bias. At the same time, there are weight caps and floors: 4% max weight per stock (8% for the top 5) and a 0.5% minimum weight. Reconstitution and rebalancing are done semi-annually, in March and September.

Portfolio

As is usual with less diversified portfolios than vanilla exposure funds, single-name concentration is high. Here, the top 10 stocks account for almost 55% of the portfolio. More importantly, Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM), Chevron Corporation (CVX), and ConocoPhillips (COP) have meaningful weights at 8.8%, 7.7%, and 7.6%, respectively, consistent with their cash flow sizes ($52 billion, $33.9 billion, and $19.8 billion, respectively).

Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) also has a meaningful weight at 6.5%, but this is where cash flow size drops to a disproportionately low level at $10.5 billion as of its last fiscal year end.

More broadly, another way to think about the exposure here before we talk about the performance of the fund is that pure upstream stocks from the top 10 names are 27% of the fund:

  • ConocoPhillips at 7.6%
  • Occidental at 6.5%
  • EOG Resources, Inc. (EOG) at 5%
  • Devon Energy Corporation (DVN) at 4%
  • Diamondback Energy, Inc. (FANG) at 3.9%

But you also have names such as Exxon Mobil and Chevron, which are integrated majors but have upstream-leaning businesses based on earnings.

This is consistent with industry distribution. Crude producers and integrated oil & gas companies account for almost half of the portfolio and 16.6%, respectively.

Performance

FTXN has tracked its benchmark as closely as you'd expect from an ETF with such a high expense ratio. That being said, the track record is so long that the return differential doesn't seem too meaningful. More specifically, as of February 27, the tracking error has been 0.16 percentage points YTD, 0.77 points over 1 year, 0.73 points annualized over 3 years, and 0.80 points annualized over 5 years.

Now, FTXN has greatly outperformed the State Street SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (XOP) over the long run. This can be explained by the fact that the latter is equal-weighted, and broad exposure like that has underperformed the quality/cash flow leaders mentioned above. It's exactly the same reason that FTXN underperformed the iShares US Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (IEO); the latter is even more E&P concentrated (75%) and has higher single-name concentration (top 10 holdings are ~70% of the portfolio).

FTXN, however, hasn't lagged IEO as much as XOP has lagged FTXN, so I view it as a very efficient way to generate attractive returns without taking too much concentration risk.

More recently, it has outperformed the market by a significant margin. Since the war in Iran started, the oil price has been an important tailwind for the fund due to its significant upstream bias.

Iran effectively blocked the Strait of Hormuz by threatening to attack any vessel that would try to pass, and the oil supply shock was very significant since roughly 20 million barrels per day moved through the strait in 2025 (about 25% of the world's seaborne oil trade).

Risks

The Israeli Foreign Minister has said that Israel effectively won the war. While the conflict can endure until Israel's "objectives are met," a resolution doesn't seem to be a long-term prospect for the situation to be a reliable tailwind for FTXN. The price of oil has played such an important role in this ETF's short-term performance that if the market starts pricing in higher supply than it currently does, the price can drop nearly as hard as it surged, undoing FTXN's recent gains.

A broader risk that should concern investors is the single-name concentration and the reliance on upstream businesses, which result in heightened volatility in times like this.

Conclusion

Overall, FTXN is a decent way to get exposure to oil & gas companies and has performed relatively well over the years. The expense ratio may seem high, but to the best of my knowledge, there isn't a close alternative with that quality bias and exposure breakdown. That being said, investors may want to give serious consideration to the risk of a near-term oil reversal because the conditions that have created the recent outperformance are fragile. Aside from a potential reversal, heightened volatility due to those conditions by itself makes an entry unattractive right now.

data.title: Gold The Safe Haven Versus Silver The Wildcard
data.description:
Gold and silver have both delivered strong returns over the past year. After initially lagging, silver prices shot up in late 2025, outpacing gold by a substantial margin. This surge prompted investors to review their precious-metals exposure and assess the similarities – and differences – between the two.

Despite gold and silver sitting under the precious metals label, their market structures and behaviours diverge sharply. Gold benefits from a more balanced demand base, deeper liquidity, and materially lower volatility. Silver, with its industrial bias and higher volatility, behaves more cyclically and is more sensitive to broader commodity flows.

These distinctions shape how each metal behaves in portfolios: gold consistently offers diversification during stress, supporting its role as a strategic, defensive asset, while silver tends to amplify moves – both up and down – acting more like a high‑beta complement than a substitute.

Chart 1: Gold has delivered strong returns, but at a much more consistent pace when compared to silver

Price performance of gold and silver spot prices*

Market structure: Contrasting supply and demand forces

Gold’s dispersed demand vs silver’s cyclical sensitivity

Gold and silver are often put into the same bucket, but each is characterised by unique attributes and drivers. One key distinction between the two is their end-demand mix.

Gold has a well-balanced dual nature, as both a consumer good and an investment asset (Chart 2). Gold is also an integral component to central bank foreign reserves, a trend that has picked up steam in recent years. And while gold investment demand dominates during periods of risk, consumer demand tends to step in during periods of economic growth. Gold’s investment share helps explain why it can behave more like a financial asset – sensitive to policy decisions and credit conditions – often supporting its role as a hedge.

Silver demand, on the other hand, is dominated by industry demand (Chart 3). The metal’s dominant industrial share increases its exposure to pro-cyclical risk sentiment – raising the odds of it trading closer to industrial metals and risky assets during periods of market stress and economic deceleration.

Chart 2: Gold has a dual nature…

Percentage of total gold demand*

Chart 3: …while silver is exposed to cyclical trends

Percentage of total silver demand*

Gold a primary product, silver a by-product

From a supply perspective, gold and silver differ sharply. Gold is predominantly mined as a primary product, while 70–80% of silver comes as a by‑product of copper, lead and zinc, making silver supply more exposed to disruptions in those sectors.1 Gold production is also more geographically diverse (Chart 4), whereas silver output is concentrated in Latin America – with Mexico (1st), Peru (3rd), Bolivia (4th) and Chile (6th) all ranking among the top ten producers.

Mexico alone producing nearly twice as much as China (2nd). This makes silver more susceptible to supply shocks.

Chart 4: Gold production is more geographically diverse

Number of top 10 producers by region

Recycling also plays a much larger role in gold supply than in silver. Gold recycling contributes roughly one‑third of global supply, while silver recycling covers only about 19% of demand.2 Because so much silver is dispersed across electronics, solar panels and industrial applications, recovery is often uneconomical. As a result, gold benefits from a more stable secondary supply, whereas silver faces structurally tighter physical market conditions.

Market dynamics: the gold market is substantially larger and more liquid

Trading volumes – gold’s trading volumes far exceed silver’s

The size and liquidity of the gold and silver markets differ markedly. Gold is a deep market with an estimated US$15 trillion in financial form – mostly physical bullion. It’s also an extremely liquid global asset, with trading activity comparable to major bond and currency markets, while silver’s market is considerably smaller.

As a proxy, we compare average daily trading volumes for the two largest US-based physically-backed ETFs for each metal as well as COMEX futures and LBMA OTC activity. Over the past five years, gold ETFs have averaged US$2.3bn per day versus US$0.7bn for silver, and the gap widens in futures (US$55bn vs. US$11bn) and OTC trading (US$97bn vs. US$13bn).

Recent averages have been lifted by a surge in metals trading late last year and into 2026, as rising prices spurred momentum‑driven activity. Through February, silver ETF volumes reached US$11.4bn - nearly nine times their FY25 average—while futures jumped to US$71bn per day (4× FY25) and OTC activity rose to US$59bn (3× FY25) (Chart 5).

Chart 5: Gold trades more than silver across the spectrum of trading instruments

Average daily trading volume of gold and silver ETFs, futures, and OTC*

Intraday spread- a clear divide in market depth

While trading activity has increased, a clearer measure of market depth is the intraday bid‑ask spread. By this metric, silver is meaningfully less liquid, with an average spread of 9bps – more than four times wider than gold’s 2bps (Chart 6). Silver spreads also spike up in periods of risk to very high levels, making it very costly for investors to initiate or liquidate positions.

Chart 6: Silver’s spread is materially higher than gold’s

Spot gold and silver intraday spread (bps)*

Volatility – silver requires a different risk budget

The by-product of wider spreads is higher volatility. Silver’s volatility is roughly twice that of gold (Chart 7).

This has direct implications for investors, particularly in risk-parity frameworks: to target equal risk contribution, the notional allocation to silver typically needs to be materially smaller than for gold.

Chart 7: Silver’s higher volatility has implications for portfolio construction

Daily annualised volatility*

Portfolio role: Same “precious metals” label, very different portfolio behaviour

Gold and silver are both categorised as precious metals because of their elemental properties and long-standing cultural significance. As a result, they are typically grouped together within major commodity benchmarks although gold generally receives a larger weighting (Charts 8 and 9). However, despite this categorisation, the two metals behave quite differently during periods of market stress and are supported by distinct demand drivers.

Chart 8: Gold and silver grouped as precious metals…

Chart 9: …gold has the larger weighting…

Gold has historically acted as the more defensive, lower-volatility diversifier, while silver tends to behave as a hybrid – part precious metal and part industrial metal – with higher volatility and greater equity sensitivity during drawdowns.

Additionally, the open-interest (OI) backdrop reinforces why broad commodity or precious metals de-risking matters more for silver than for gold. The combined open interest in broad commodity index futures and precious-metals futures as a share of gold’s own futures open interest is only 1.2%. This is much larger for silver at 6.4%, making silver more vulnerable to index-led selloffs (Chart 10).

Chart 10: …but silver is more index sensitive than gold

Gold is a “true” diversifier

For portfolios seeking crisis diversification, gold’s negative correlation to the stock market during drawdowns underpins its role as a hedge. Silver does not consistently provide the same protection: in risk-off environments it often behaves more like a cyclical asset, limiting its stabilising role (Chart 11).

Chart 11: Gold provides diversification during crisis

Equity correlation to gold, silver and industrial metals*

As a result, incorporating gold into a diversified portfolio has historically improved risk-adjusted returns more than an equivalent allocation to silver (Chart 12).3

Chart 12: Gold delivers better risk-adjusted returns compared to silver

Risk-adjusted returns of a hypothetical portfolio with and without gold or silver*

Contagion dynamics: gold shows remarkable immunity to silver contagion

Silver’s beta to gold has been consistently above 1 over the past two decades, underscoring its tendency to exceed gold’s directional moves. And while silver’s sensitivity is higher in periods when gold is falling than when gold is rising, its long-term average beta clusters at around 1.3. This underscores silver’s behaviour as a higher octane precious metal – amplifying gold’s moves to both the upside and downside – and reinforces why investors often view it as a tactical complement to strategic gold allocations rather than a substitute (Chart 13).

Chart 13: Silver’s sensitivity to gold is high and rises even when gold is falling

1-year rolling silver beta to gold*

Moreover, our analysis shows that gold appears to lead silver at short horizons (1-minute frequency), while silver does not provide a comparable leading signal for gold (Table 1). This asymmetry is consistent with gold acting as the primary “information anchor” in precious metals – absorbing macro shocks first – while silver behaves more like a higher-beta satellite that tends to adjust following moves in gold. In other words, if silver was to suffer due to a cyclical downturn, it would not necessarily spread to the gold market.

Table 1: Silver is a price-taker relative to gold, not a price-setter

Granger‑causality analysis on gold and silver, controlling for the US dollar*

Test Direction 1 minute lag
Gold → Silver *** = significant at the 1% level (p < 0.01)
Silver → Gold Not significant

*Results report Granger-causality F tests from a bivariate VAR estimated on intraday returns from 31 December 1999 to 27 February 2026 for gold and silver. “Gold → Silver” tests the null that lagged gold returns do not predict silver returns and vice versa for “Silver → Gold.” *** = significant at the 1% level (p < 0.01). BIC = Lag length 1 minute. Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council.

In summary

Gold and silver may be considered precious metals, but they are diametrically different in their market structure and portfolio behaviour. Gold’s more balanced demand mix and substantially deeper liquidity support tighter spreads, lower volatility and more consistent diversification – particularly during equity drawdowns. Silver’s more industrial-heavy demand base, smaller market depth and wider spreads contribute to higher volatility and more cyclical performance, while also making it more sensitive to commodity index flows. Taken together, these factors suggest that silver can complement gold tactically for investors seeking a higher beta alternative, but it is not a like-for-like substitute for gold’s strategic role.

Footnotes

1Which countries produce the world’s silver? | 24 January 2025.

2Recycling contribution to supply in 2025, data from the Silver Institute 2025 Survey.

3Portfolio: 40% MSCI World Index, 5% MSCI EM Index, 5% MSCI Global Small Cap Index, 20% Bloomberg US Treasury, 15% Bloomberg US Corporate Index, 5% Bloomberg US High Yield Index, 3.3% FTSE REITs Index, 3.3% HFRI Hedge Fund Index, 3.3% FTSE Private Equity Index.

data.title: Broadcom: The 'Utility' Of The AI Era
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Broadcom (AVGO) is no longer a company purely in the semiconductor business. Our investment thesis centers around its evolution into a "digital public utility" by dominating the two most critical bottlenecks in AI, which are ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) design and ultra-high-speed networking. Together with its VMware software, the company is now a comprehensive AI company with both semiconductor networking and subscription software businesses.

Therefore, when giants are known for their LLMs and GPUs, Broadcom is providing the underlying infrastructure. This is the reason behind management's bold guidance of $100 billion revenue in 2027 for just the chips business alone - alongside the "sharp accelerated growth" in other business segments.

Therefore, with such positioning in the AI supply chain, every time a giant like Google or Meta wants to enhance its AI products, Broadcom gets paid. With its growing subscription software segment, it hedges against the overcapacity in the chip supply chain when the days come. This is why I am giving it a Buy.

The Benefits of Custom Silicon (ASIC)

Broadcom's revenue in AI has doubled to reach $8.4 billion in 2026 Q1, in which the custom XPU segment has grown by more than 140%. Its impressive clients include Google, Meta, Anthropic, OpenAI, and others. The question here is why would cash-rich AI companies want to let Broadcom earn from them while they have the capability to do it themselves?

The answer can be separated between "physical reality" and "cost of trial and error."

First of all, within a modern AI ASIC, the "AI Brain" that AI giants are responsible for designing represents only 30-40% of the silicon area. The remaining 60-70% consists of "infrastructure pipes." It includes critical components like high-speed data lanes, PCIe interfaces, and memory controllers. Therefore, Broadcom owns and specializes in the IPs that are already verified to work on TSMC’s latest 2nm/3nm processes, which are the majority of the ASICs, and it takes more than 5 years if another company would like to do it in-house. This gives Broadcom the moat in ASIC chips.

From a financial standpoint, the shift toward Broadcom's ASICs is an enhancement in efficiency. General-purpose GPUs (like NVIDIA’s) are "jacks-of-all-trades" containing "Dark Silicon" — transistors that remain idle during specific AI tasks but still leak power and generate heat. On the other hand, by partnering with Broadcom, giants like Google and Meta can get rid of the inefficiency, allowing their chips to achieve 30% to 50% higher throughput for the same power budget. This significantly lowers the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and the "Cost per Token," providing a massive competitive advantage in model deployment.

On the other hand, hyperscalers like Google and Meta's strength lies in their logical design, which they know how the information flow is the most efficient and how much SRAM they need. However, the challenge to them is to convert these to a blueprint that can be produced with TSMC's 3nm and even 2nm chips. With that, Broadcom is the most famous for such design and production.

Inorganic Growth of Broadcom

Current CEO of the Company Hock Tan is not a typical engineer - he has a strong background with a Harvard MBA and private equity and investment experience. Therefore, in the past 10+ years, the company has done a couple of acquisitions and mergers to reinforce the moat of Broadcom's core business while serving the top clients in the AI space.

One of the most critical acquisitions was VMware, a new cash-generating segment for Broadcom. In Q1 2026, software revenue reached $6.8 billion, with VMware’s Annual Recurring Revenue growing by 19% year-over-year. This segment is no longer a secondary business but a high-margin "cash machine" that fundamentally boosts Broadcom's valuation from a cyclical chipmaker to a digital, recurring business.

The software division now operates at a 93% gross margin and a 78% operating margin. For investors, this offers additional comforts and de-risking that while semiconductor sales can be volatile, VMware’s subscriptions provide stable and recurring growth in free cash flow, which reached $8 billion in Q1 alone.

The Worries From Recent Earnings

The question now remains, what financial value do these strengths of Broadcom bring to its investors?

From the metrics, this earnings report is an impressive one:

  • Total revenue reached $19.3 billion (YoY growth 29%)
  • AI revenue reached a staggering $8.4 billion (YoY growth 106%), indicating that AI-related business accounts for the critical role in its business
  • Free Cash Flow reached $8 billion in one quarter alone, indicating an impressive cash-conversion ability
  • Non-GAAP EPS reached $2.05, beating Wall Street estimates

Most importantly, the guidance from its CEO clearly points out that its annual AI chip-related revenue will reach $100 billion in 2027 - a significant guidance that no CEO dares to give so easily. In fact, his guidance is backed by many of its clients and suppliers' promises, such as Anthropic's 3 GW order and OpenAI's GW-level order.

Valuation

Broadcom’s valuation is now increasingly defined by its parallel growth in both AI-related business and subscription business.

AI-related revenue surged to $8.4 billion, driven by $5.6 billion in XPU sales and $2.8 billion in networking. With Q2 projections suggesting even faster acceleration, the company has the ability to double its annual AI revenue to over $45 billion (as hinted by its CEO, supported by the orders), while I expect the software division to grow at 10% to reach $29.7 billion. Adding the non-AI business, which is now a comparatively minor segment, in 2026 Broadcom can potentially hit $90 billion in revenue.

Applying a lower forward P/S ratio given the macro environment, peer valuation ratios, as well as the concern over the end of the AI cycle, Broadcom should be valued at around 18-20x P/S. This translates into a share price range between $337 and $375, which is slightly higher than the current level from a more conservative point of view.

Investment Risks

  • High customer concentration: If giants like Google or Meta suddenly reduce AI capital expenditures, the company’s revenue guidance and business performance could be impacted.
  • New technology pathway: NVIDIA’s push into networking via Spectrum-X and NVLink Fusion threatens Broadcom’s Ethernet dominance.
  • Competition in the software business: VMware now pursues a higher-end strategy, which may drive smaller enterprises to migrate to competitors.

Conclusion

All in all, Broadcom plays a critical yet underappreciated role in the AI industry, which may not be known by all investors. With its expanding product suite and improving business and financial performance, it appears to be a safe and undervalued investment opportunity in the current volatile market.

data.title: Five Below: Why I Changed My Mind And See 65% Total Return By 2029 (Upgrade)
data.description:
A few days ago, I was reading about rumors that Apple (AAPL) was going to launch new products even more premium than the existing ones, and if you follow the news, you saw that Apple also announced a new MacBook (check it here) to compete with other entry-level laptops. So the company is targeting both ends of the market. Apple also wants to ensure high end products for those who can still pay for this “luxury,” and see no problems in paying thousands of dollars for an Apple product (as long as it is more exclusive and etc.).

You must be wondering why I am talking about Apple in an article about Five Below, Inc. (FIVE). Well, that is because what I observed about Apple is not an isolated fact. Products that were previously aimed at the middle consumer (middle class or upper middle class) are needing to adapt, since this “middle” has become more squeezed, that is, due to inflation, debt, or income under pressure for other reasons, the middle class now looks for cheaper alternatives (or skips the middle category and goes straight to a more premium product, especially for those who were already at the wealthier end or managed to ascend in some way).

And this goes very much in line with Five Below. Categorically, it is a dollar store, and management in Q4 reaffirmed that “looking ahead,” FIVE is focused on delivering “trend-right merchandise at exceptional value,” and the financials prove (and a lot) all of this. With this, I am giving an upgrade to my last rating but with a cautious view due to the less asymmetric valuation.

Five Below: Q4 Impressed Me A Lot

It’s been about 1 year since I covered Five Below. You can find the complete coverage here. I didn't think the company was bad, actually, I thought it was better than the peers in several aspects. And with my old assumptions, I even came to find a P/E of 14x for 2027, which at the time I didn't find compelling enough for a Buy rating.

One year later, it’s clear that I was wrong. The 14x earnings in 2027 (2 years ahead from 2025) were more than enough to have a margin of safety. But it’s worth mentioning that it wasn't something so obvious, actually, I think not even management expected that the results would be so good and that Five Below would grow so much in this period.

In Q4, the stock jumped more than 10% and this shows the market's surprise. And seeing the results, these ~10% gains even seem like little. Five Below opened 14 stores in Q4. It’s not an extraordinary number, but it’s a growth of 8.5% in units YoY.

But what impresses, is that revenue grew 24.3%. The difference between top line growth and unit growth is precisely caused by comparable sales (or same store sales). Comp sales in Q4 was 15.4%. As I follow other retailers, and lately I've been looking at many companies in the QSR (quick service restaurant) industry, this number really stands out. While many are struggling to maintain a stable number or in the low single digits, Five Below manages to “boast” this mid to high teens.

Of course, due to seasonality, Q4 is a stronger quarter, but the comparison here is YoY, and this shows that these operational and financial gains were not only due to seasonality, they were also due to management's strategy.

Just for comparison purposes, the adj. net margin of 2025 was 7.5%, while that of Q4 was almost 14%. I don't think Five Below will manage to deliver a consolidated net margin of 14% anytime soon, but if in a few years it manages to deliver something close to 10% - 12%, it will already be an absurd generation of shareholder value.

As for the guidance, Five Below expects something close to $5.25 billion for the top line and an adj. EPS between $7.74 and $8.25. This surpassed the market expectations which were already high. The market estimate for EPS after positive revisions in the last weeks is at ~$8.24 for 2026, and for sales it is at $5.32 billion.

In short, Five Below’s Q4 was an excellent quarter, without much to complain about.

Five Below: Awesome Tailwinds

The future of Five Below also seems promising to me, not only due to the practically perfect execution of management in the last quarters but also due to external factors. Think of the two trends I mentioned at the beginning of the article, actually, that management mentioned in the earnings release: extreme value, but “trend-right merchandise.”

These two trends connect a lot. Think that before when there was more disposable income and households were less committed, giving a Lego as a gift was more reasonable. Today there are Lego sets that cost $300, but even thinking of one for $80, it’s still expensive. And Five Below works as an “extreme value” alternative for that. I visited a store in Omaha, and there are so many gadgets, toys and several trending items like squishmellows, that work as a gift or even for a personal purchase, and instead of spending $80 on a Lego Set, the consumer can buy $20 in items at Five Below.

Items that are no less interesting, and the curation and execution of Five Below matters a lot in this context. Today the consumer is very connected and also influenced by trends on TikTok, Instagram. So having these items that are red-hot, making partnerships with influencers, many times is a marketing not only direct (in the case of paid partnerships), but also an organic marketing which is the most valuable there is.

Five Below: Valuation and Risks

With execution + tailwinds, it’s possible to have conviction that good numbers will continue appearing in the next few years. Let's say that comp sales will be 5% per year until 2030, this would be the top of the range that management stipulated for 2026. As for net new stores, let's assume 150 per year until 2030.

With this, Five Below’s revenue would be around $8.5 billion, a very high jump.

To close this equation, we need the adj. net margin. With gradual advances, which is what we are observing now, I believe Five Below can reach 10%. This in a base/optimistic scenario. That is, for 2030, the P/E would be 15x.

If in 2029 the market assumes that a fair forward P/E for FIVE is about 25x, we are talking about an implicit upside of 65% in this period, about 4 years. This gives us a CAGR of 13%, which seems like a reasonable enough total return for this case.

What leaves me more optimistic, is that these numbers are good, but they are not excellent. If next year Five Below delivers a same store sales of high single digits, and a net margin of 9%, this already changes the comparative base a lot, and makes the potential for 2030 much greater than what we are projecting now. That is why I think it’s worth a buy rating. In the base scenario the total return is good enough, but there are potentials for positive surprises.

But even so, I need to mention that this total return of ~10% - 13% per year is not that guaranteed. This is a market quite sensitive to trends, not only trends of items and that management will need to continue getting right, but also macroeconomic factors.

Besides that, we need to mention that Five Below is practically breaking paradigms here. An adj. net margin of 10% in 2030 for this type of business is achievable, but it is not the normal. Peers like Dollar General (DG) and Target (TGT) are delivering an EBITDA margin lower than 8%, actually, value stores with so much physical presence that manage to deliver numbers much better than that are exceptions, like Ross Stores (ROST) which delivers an EBITDA margin of 14% and a net margin of ~9.5%.

And this I am not praising Five Below. Of course this is great for the shareholder, but it also opens a sense of doubt. As we are projecting a gradual increase for the margin, if in a bad year this returns to "normal” or returns to the industry average, we are talking that the shareholder value in 2030 will be lower than the table above, and consequently, the total return will also be much lower.

Bottom Line

In short, Five Below’s result was very good in Q4, and I don't think it’s just a passing moment. I believe that execution and these tailwinds should manage to maintain a gradual growth in the next few years, and with that, continue unlocking value. But as the market cap has already risen a lot, my Buy rating comes along with this caveat of caution, because mainly looking at multiples for the next years, FIVE already trades at a premium relative to peers (deservedly, see the table below) and whether one likes it or not, this opens a potential for de-rating in case something goes wrong.

data.title: Agnico Eagle: A Gold Standard With A 'Platinum' Premium
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Currently, high price volatility continues to characterize the gold and gold mining stock markets, fueling debate over the dilemma: Should investors chase record returns from gold miners, or wait for a more opportune moment to buy assets following a deep technical correction? Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM)—one of the most stable companies in the world—not only has assets in the safest jurisdictions but also boasts competitive production costs, which allow it to generate record free cash flow. The year 2025 proved to be transformative for the company, with the implementation of current projects extending the mines’ operational life into 2030–2040. The presence of unique regional synergies and strict cost control have turned AEM into a powerful "cash cow." The valuation of forward multiples, compared to other major gold producers (Newmont Corporation, AngloGold, and Barrick Mining), indicates a high premium on the stock price. This article aims to provide a detailed analysis of whether this premium is justified. It also discusses what course of action investors should take if it is not, with a view to further adding AEM to their growth portfolio.

AEM's Strategy and Growth Drivers

Now, it's clear that AEM has cemented its status as one of the most stable and largest players in the global gold market. It had a record year across a number of metrics made possible by scaling up its operations and focusing on assets in safe jurisdictions (Canada, Australia, Finland). Unlike many of its competitors (especially Barrick), AEM minimizes its geopolitical and regulatory risks, which may prove critical in the long term. As part of its strategy, its main pillar is regional synergy, since the company owns a unique network of mines and processing facilities in the Abitibi Belt. Thanks to this, the supply chains are optimized, and a common infrastructure has been established for various deposits. Consequently, by 2026, it is expected that AISC will range from $1,400 to $1,555 per ounce, ensuring greater business margins at current gold prices.

As part of its key objectives for the coming decade, the company aims to scale up gold production by 20–30%, thereby bringing total annual output to 4 million ounces. There are a number of factors that could serve as key drivers of growth in achieving this ambitious goal. The first is the implementation of the Odyssey project, one of the largest underground mines in the world. As part of this project, initial production has begun at the East Gouldie site, while the full commissioning of the project will turn it into a "cash cow" for the coming decade. Secondly, AEM has accelerated its investment in the development of the underground section of the Detour Lake mine (planning to attract $200 million in investment by mid-2027), the goal of which is to increase gold production to 1 million ounces by 2030. As well as expanding underground mining, they're also optimizing the processing capacity linked to this asset, so they can boost processing to 29 million tons a year by 2028.

Thirdly, there's active exploration underway at the high-potential Hope Bay mine, estimated to have the potential to increase annual gold production to 400,000 ounces. The Upper Beaver project is also underway, the current development of which will increase production of not only gold but also copper. According to estimates, potential gold production is expected to reach 200,000–220,000 ounces per year, with copper production at approximately 3,600 metric tons per year. Separately, the active development of the copper segment is worth noting, since it can diversify AEM’s product portfolio. In particular, the joint venture with Teck Resources in Mexico—San Nicolás—is expected to begin producing copper and zinc in 2026. The annual copper output is projected to reach 63,000 tons by 2030. The partnership with another company, Kinross, allows for the development of the Manh Choh asset in Alaska, which is rich in copper ore processed at the Fort Knox facility. The latter project is significant as it increases cash flow generation with minimal capital expenditure. AEM's portfolio currently includes a record gold reserve of 55.4 million ounces, thanks to the implementation of new projects and ongoing active exploration. By comparison, the gold reserve stood at approximately 45 million ounces as recently as 2021.

AEM's Assets

While I've described the company's key projects above, giving a quick overview of AEM's portfolio, here I'll go into more detail about its current assets. Like I mentioned, one of the company's main competitive strengths is its regional integration, since a bunch of mines are connected by shared infrastructure, reducing operating costs and making logistics easier. We'll split the deposits into two groups (Canadian and international).

This first group is the foundation of the company’s production scale and includes Canada’s largest mines. One of the largest is Detour Lake, whose gold reserves stand at 18.6 million ounces. A second mine, Canadian Malartic Complex, encompasses the Odyssey project, set to fill the excess capacity of an existing processing plant with a daily throughput of 40,000 tons. Yet another asset in Quebec, LaRonde Complex, has a high-tech mine that allows for the extraction of gold, silver, zinc, and copper at a depth of over 3 km. Apart from Quebec, AEM also extracts gold in the Arctic region of Canada. Regardless of the operational and logistical challenges, it's a resource-rich region. Meadowbank/Amaruq makes the largest contribution to production from this region, with Meliadine considered the most profitable mine. It's expected to remain in operation until 2043. Hope Bay is another mine poised to become a future star, with the potential to produce up to 400,000 ounces of gold annually, which I have already noted.

This second group consists of mines in Australia and Finland. Despite the fact that these mines are located in stable jurisdictions, ongoing geopolitical risks could disrupt supply chains. Fosterville is the most marginal mine, with production of around 150,000–160,000 ounces of gold. Deep exploration is actively underway there to discover new gold deposits, given that peak production previously reached 600,000 ounces per year. With a stable annual output of approximately 200,000 ounces of gold, Kittilä is considered the largest mine in Europe. The mine's capacity will be expanded in 2026 with a new hoisting system, increasing the efficiency of operations. As such, a very well-balanced portfolio of assets makes AEM as secure, profitable, and efficient as possible.

AEM's Financial Profile

AEM's financial profile is impressive, demonstrating high stability in its results, a strong level of resilience, as well as its status as a "cash cow." Expenses are kept under tight control, contributing to the company's profitability alongside rising gold prices. Without a doubt, by 2025, AEM had undergone a series of transformations that enabled explosive profit growth. As a result, by 2025, the net profit amounted to $4.461 billion, up 135% from 2024. Adjusted net income for Q4 2025 reached a record $1.351 billion with an average AISC of $1,517, while revenue increased by 59.6% to $3.56 billion. Projections for 2026 indicate that EPS is expected to grow by up to 68% (if the economic conditions for gold remain positive).

As for AEM's capital expenditures, investment of $2.175 billion to $2.395 billion is projected for 2026. The capex figures are reasonable, taking into account that free cash flow for 2025 reached a record $4.399 billion. Meanwhile, cash flow from operations amounted to $6.817 billion. Due to this cash generation, it's able to finance all capital expenditures from its own resources, pay off existing debt, and increase shareholder payouts. By the way, this transition to a net cash position by the end of 2025 can be considered the main achievement in AEM’s financial profile. Company liquidity consists of $2.87 billion in cash, with net cash amounting to $2.67 billion. Generating cash flow allowed the company to actively pay down its debt throughout 2025, as a result of which the debt now poses no significant risks. In the future, I would expect an increase in shareholder payouts; at the beginning of 2026, the quarterly dividend was already raised by 12.5% to $0.45 per share. Dividend yield stands at just 0.86%, a modest figure compared to market yields. Yet, the company managed to return a total of $1.4 billion to shareholders in 2025 alone. Apart from dividends, it also engages in share buybacks, and this approach will continue into 2026.

Valuation of AEM Shares

I suggest comparing a number of traditional forward-looking multiples (GAAP P/E, EV/EBITDA, and EV/sales) to the major competitors, the largest issuers among the "gold stocks": Newmont Corporation (NEM), AngloGold Ashanti (AU), and Barrick Mining Corporation (B). Comparing the multiples makes it clear that AEM is overvalued relative to median values.

According to the median P/E ratio, shares in AEM are overvalued by 30.81%; based on the median EV/EBITDA ratio, the overvaluation is 35.23%; and based on the median EV/Sales ratio, it is 39.17%. The average premium across all three multiples comes to 35.07%. Ultimately, there's potential for AEM shares to be overvalued compared to the median among the largest "gold stocks." However, the positive economic outlook for the gold market—which suggests further growth for "gold stocks" in 2026—is limited by this factor. That said, when looking at the growth dynamics of all these gold stocks over the past year, AEM shows the lowest growth. In comparison with AU, its performance was half as strong.

AEM Buy Zone

I really like the company’s business strategy, assets structure, and financial profile, but the valuation based on key multiples shows a significant premium of 35%. That’s why my recommendation for AEM is a temporary "Hold" with the potential to upgrade to "Buy." In my plans, it's to purchase shares of this company after the technical correction phase has been completed. Clearly, a decline of up to 35% should not be expected. It's too deep a pullback, in which I do not believe. Nevertheless, correction of up to 10–15% is quite possible, given the current high volatility of the gold market and gold stocks. Take a look at the analysis of the AEM daily chart to identify a point of interest for opening a long position. There's currently a support zone ($187–$204) that serves as a zone of interest. At current prices, a correction to the lower boundary of the support zone would represent a 10% decline, sufficient for opening an initial long position. In addition, there's a 100-day moving average ($195) within this support zone, providing an additional factor to prevent the price from falling into a deeper technical correction. As such, for me, the price range of $187–$195 would be an attractive level to buy AEM.

Risks Associated with AEM

While AEM has a sound financial profile and an effective business strategy centered on high-margin mining operations, a number of specific risks must be taken into account.

  • Firstly, gold price volatility remains a significant market risk, because in the event of a sharp market correction, AEM's share price would decline at a higher rate relative to the market.
  • Secondly, the inflationary risks may have a partially negative impact, increasing the cost of gold production and, as a result, impacting the business's margins. Moreover, potential inflationary pressures could lead to an increase in Fed interest rates, thereby reducing the attractiveness of investments in gold mining stocks (as some capital flows into the bond market).
  • Thirdly, operating risks are associated with deep underground gold mining at AEM’s flagship projects. Unexpected seismic activity or any accidents could cause significant delays that would have a material impact on gold production volumes.
  • Finally, risks may arise from challenges in the transformation of Detour Lake, as the transition from open-pit mining to large-scale underground mining requires costly investments and complex processes to adjust the operational cycle. Missing the project timeline or exceeding capex will worsen the free cash flow forecast for 2026–2028.

Conclusion

Given the results of my fundamental analysis of AEM’s business and financial profiles, my recommendation would be to buy this stock. But for now, my rating is "Hold," since the company has a high premium in forward multiples relative to its main competitors among the largest gold miners. With that said, my personal buy limit order is set in the $187–$195 range. In my view, AEM represents the most reliable major gold producer in the world, one that's striving for operational excellence and record-breaking scale. Among the company's strengths are a portfolio of highly profitable and reliable assets, increasing dividends and a share buyback program, low regulatory risks, transition to net cash status, and expectations of record EPS growth of 68% in 2026.

data.title: Vale: The Biggest Bargain In The Mining Sector
data.description:
Introduction

Vale S.A. (VALE) has been on my review list for a while, as I’ve covered most of its peers like Rio Tinto (RIO) and Freeport-McMoRan (FCX). The firm is extremely cheap considering its fundamental transformation that has been under-recognized by the broad market. Despite a positive macro landscape and a nice run-up in share prices, I believe that the company can still shoot higher with its best-in-class cost structure.

Operation Overview

The largest engine behind Vale’s cash flow remains its iron ore operations, which for the full year delivered the strongest performance since the pre-Brumadinho era. Production has reached 336MM tons, surpassing the guidance and resulting in a 2% Y/Y increase. This also highlights Vale’s overall competitive advantage in its integrated logistics infrastructure, with the Carajas and Vitoria a Minas railroads, which connect primary mining hubs to deep-water ports capable of hosting Valemax vessels, the largest bulk carriers in the world. This integration creates a massive barrier to entry and a cost advantage.

As such, the C1 cash costs reached $21.3 per ton, a 2% Y/Y reduction which marks the second consecutive year of deflation in an otherwise inflationary global environment. Management has also guided for further improvements, targeting a potential $20 per ton. As such, the AISC for Iron ore has reached $54.2 per ton in 2025, while realized prices have averaged $95.4 per ton, resulting in Vale’s high margin profile.

The global steel industry has also pivoted toward decarbonization as Chinese steel mills need to improve blast furnace productivity, shifting towards high-grade iron ore making Carajas ore the benchmark for high-quality feed. That’s why the share of premium brands in Vale’s portfolio has risen significantly and Carajas sales are forecasted to grow to 50MM tons in 2026, allowing Vale to capture the premium segment, decoupling earnings from the 62% Fe.

Though Vale is largely known for Iron ore, it has expanded into what is called Vale Base Metals, where the firm is undergoing a multibillion dollar roadmap to push Vale into the copper and nickel markets, which have already seen the segment’s EBIDTA more than double in Q4 to $1.4B.

Current Dynamics

One of the most prevalent bearish signals has been the decline of the Chinese property sector. It continues to struggle as steel demand from this segment is expected to drop to 837MM metric tons in 2026. More so, the Chinese government has set a GDP growth target of 4.5% to 5% with a record 7 trillion yuan allocated to infrastructure and public services. This investment will be less steel-intensive per dollar than residential property, but it actually provides a critical floor for iron ore demand that has been a big question mark over the past couple of years.

The Middle East situation is also drastically affecting Vale's underlying operations as it increases the prices of energy and shipping. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz means that vessels have to change routes and add weeks to their transit times whilst diesel prices also soared thus affecting mining equipment. For example, rates on the Santos to Qingdao route have increased by 57% on a Y/Y basis.

Valuation

Vale is undeniably cheap, as all of its major valuation metrics are at the bottom of the peer group, as highlighted by its P/E Non-GAAP of 8.01, drastically lower than BHP (BHP) at 17.51 and Rio Tinto at 14.54. The same could be said with Vale’s EV/EBITDA of 5.55, which Is less than half of Glencore’s levels and Southern Copper (SCCO)'s as well. Even on a Price to book view, Vale is the only one trading below 2, while on a Free cash flow point of view, Vale currently has a fair yield of 4.6%. This discount is persisting despite Vale regaining its position as the world’s largest iron ore producer, as it surpassed RIO in total output by the end of 2025. This discount has pushed the company to have a massive dividend yield that is currently hovering at 8% while the 4-year average dividend yield is standing at 10.28%.

Regarding the firm’s growth prospects, I’m enthusiastic about the Novo Carajas copper expansion, which aims to double copper production capacity to around 700,000 tons per year by 2035, up from the current levels of 382,000 tons over the past year. On the iron side, Vale is planning to reach 360MM tons by 2030 with a focus on 65% Fe ore that will allow it to reduce emissions and improve blast furnace efficiency.

Vale is also repositioning its nickel business to benefit from the EV transition, targeting a production increase to 210-250 kt by 2030. The company will aim for its nickel segment to reach cash flow neutrality by early 2027. There is also the possibility of an IPO of the VBM section as early as mid-2026 that could unlock the valuation behind the high-growth copper and nickel assets.

The core business of Vale remains highly profitable as highlighted by the pro forma net income of $7.8B for the full year, a 28% Y/Y increase, while recurring FCF remained robust at $4.8B for the year, thanks to higher volumes and lower costs in the iron ore segment.

Risks

At the end of the day, Vale’s business is dependent on iron ore prices which are influenced by the Chinese economy. While infrastructure spending provides a floor, the weakness in residential remains a major drag. If prices for iron ores (SCO:COM) decline toward the $80 range, the dividend capacity would be significantly compressed.

There is also the regulatory risk coming from the headquarters in Brazil. The current Lula administration has signaled a desire for tighter taxation on the mining industry. Recently, the government levied a 12% export tax on crude oil and I might fear that these measures might be extended to the mining sector. A new regulatory framework for mining is also stalled in the Brazilian Congress, creating uncertainty regarding future permitting.

Conclusion

I believe that the convergence of dynamics makes Vale a solid buy, considering that it shed the overhang of the Mariana situation and it remains one of the most efficient producers of high-grade iron ore. The company also has credible growth avenues in copper and nickel, proving that Vale can diversify away from iron that should push for a re-rating of the mining company. I rate Vale as a Buy.
