data.title: Galiano Gold: From Weak Quarter To Early Recovery Signals
data.description:
Since June 2025, when I analyzed Galiano Gold (GAU), the stock rose around 70%. I attribute this increase to the gold price, and part of it goes to the company. As of now, the company is coming out of the stagnation phase and is moving towards a clear production breakout. At the time I covered it, the main question was whether the growth was going to be real or if it was just another promise in the mining sector.

After Q1 2025 results, the situation was looking a little bad. The production was going down, AISC started growing, and headline numbers did not impress. Now, when looking at it deeper, it seems to me that this was not weakness but a transition phase, which most of the time is looking worse than it is. In other words, at the time I believed it was a bad quarter; now it is becoming a better situation, and the company is transitioning towards better operations. I believe it should pay off in a few years.

What Changed?

Since my last article, the narrative did not change, to be fair. But now we have the first real numbers, which let us see it not just in theory. Q1 results, when I covered them, looked weak; it was 20k oz production and AISC above $2,000. This, at first sight, was making me anxious about rating it as a buy. Now the numbers are showing a very different story, which I believe in more. Q4 2025 production is now around 37k oz, and AISC decreased to $2,033, compared to $2,283 in the previous quarter. In fact, gold production increased 15% QoQ, and AISC declined by 11%. This is a clear signal that operational bottlenecks started to disappear and the problematic asset is coming back to a normal regime.

As you can see, this is already affecting cash flows from operations. In Q4, operating cash flow reached $55.8 million and revenue reached $159.7 million. This is a very good result given that production did not reach its full potential just yet. In my opinion, this is a moment when the narrative, or what we can call the story, is transitioning. I believe that exactly this transition in mining stocks is often undervalued by the market.

In my opinion, the market is overlooking that the story is real now and believes that this is just volatility. In reality, this is a highly planned-out operational transformation. All this story is not revolving around whether the asset is good or not (we already know it is). Now we should ask whether the company can quickly go from the stripping-heavy phase to the normal production stage. In the last few quarters, we saw that the biggest pressure came from stripping, which was necessary to reach higher grade zones. Naturally it tends to blow AISC out of proportion and decrease short-term production, which happened to GAU. Why is it good? It is an investment into higher margins in the future. Now we have signs that this is nearing the end, and operations should stabilize.

From the operation side, we have a secondary crusher installation, which is a critical moment and a catalyst. Until now, I believed that one crusher was not enough and was causing a bottleneck. When this component will spring into action, throughput should increase without additional fixed cost pressure. This should go directly into margin expansion, but this is my opinion, so do not take it for granted, as I am speculating. Also, the company is moving to higher-grade zones, especially in Abore and Nkran. Even at current throughput, I think that recovered ounces should grow. I now believe more and more in their strengthening. At first, yes, it was skeptical that they were sacrificing short-term performance. Now they've unlocked a stronger cash flow, which is also decreasing some risks, in my opinion.

Can They Execute?

In short, yes. In Q4 the company reported having $108.3 million in cash, and in the presentation (I have added it above), they also have a $75 million undrawn revolving credit facility. To be fair, this is a strong safety cushion if you ask me. To me, when investing in a junior, it is not about the cash number but the transitions, which can be financed internally. I like it because it rules out a possible new share issuance, at least the chances of it happening. Operating cash flow helps sustain this, and I really like it; many junior producers tend to have problems when in the same situation, but GAU shows resilience.

I analyze a lot of mining companies, and this is very important, because a lot of mining stories fall apart not because of geology, not even because of costs. It most of the time happens when a company does not have time to enter the market and enters the market with a bad equity raise. Here it seems we should not have this risk for now. With more than $100 million in cash and strong cash flow, the company actually looks in a good and strong position to me. I believe that dilution risk decreases, and it gives a good setup for an execution story.

P.S. I know that we should not call this company a junior because it is producing, but methodologically speaking, it is a junior producer.

Valuation

As I mentioned before, I believe that the company is undervalued. If we look at current metrics, the difference between Galiano and peers is huge. It does not explain the risk that it poses at all. We can see that the company is traded at around 2.9x FY2 P/E and 3.7x P/E, when at the same time, names like Equinox or Eldorado are near the 8-11x range. The peers I have mentioned do not really have the same turnaround potential that GAU shows. The same can be seen through EV/EBITDA: looking forward, Galiano is around 1.9x, when peers are sitting at 3.5 and 5.5. That means that the market is still trading GAU as a problem asset.

Most importantly, this discount exists even when we see the first signs of recovery. In other words, I think that the market is still pricing in the weakness it showed a few quarters back, and I think it's wrong. Also, I believe gold prices currently are dragging miners down, as they tend to react more violently to short-term gold moves. I believe that the stock is undervalued for its dropping AISC and increasing production.

Summary

All in all, this story is now clearer than it was when I covered their Q1. It is not a perfect setup, because execution remains one of the risks, and one asset dependency did not disappear. However, at least now we see the first signals that operational transition works. Production is stabilizing, costs are decreasing, and financial health is intact. The biggest question today still remains as it did in Q1. Will they be able to normalize the operations properly? In my opinion, the market is pricing in old narratives, not the trajectory we have. That is why I see upside and am maintaining the rating as a buy.

data.title: Hello Group: Searing Overseas Growth Likely To Maintain 'Higher Lows' Trend
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on Hello Group Inc. (MOMO) in December of last year when we stood pat on our 'Hold' rating in the Beijing-based operator. To recap, MOMO operates in the mobile-based social and entertainment space through various segments, where its traditional MOMO app remains the largest by revenue stream for the company.

As noted in December, management's pitch is to convince investors that this company's future is oriented around the softening (and eventual leveling out) of the company's MOMO app, coupled with searing growth in international markets. We pointed out at the time that management is banking on the success (outside the mainland Chinese market) of the likes of the company's Soulchill, Amar, and Yaahlan offerings to ultimately convince investors that this company's turnaround is for real.

However, poor growth numbers in the company's Q3 earnings release last December essentially rubberstamped our 'Hold' rating at the time. Remember, although management continues to promote the cash-cow nature of the MOMO domestic app (even in a declining market), declining growth means fewer resources being available to mirror something like the success 'Soulchill' has achieved in recent times, all things remaining equal.

Suffice it to say, the 10% drop in domestic revenue in Q3 (irrespective of overseas growth), coupled with margin pressure, led us to believe that lower lows were in store for the stock. With shares trading at approximately $6.69 in our December post Q3 commentary, shares kept making lower lows until March of this year, dropping below $6 a share in the process.

Improved Profitability In Q4

In saying the above, the company's impressive Q4 and full-year release (3/18/2025) has the capacity to finally put a floor in this stock, in our view. We state this because operating margins performed significantly better in Q4 (11.92%) compared to the same period of 12 months prior. Furthermore, the fact that new tax policies indirectly helped the Momo platform in the latter part of the year (literally forced management to focus on the small ticket-spender), resulted in strong Momo-app gains in fresh paying users.

Therefore, all eyes are now on whether the post-Q4-earnings bump can hold on the technical chart, potentially signaling a long-term ascending triangle in play and confirmation that the company's structural pivot is finally gaining traction.

Running The Numbers Demonstrates Encouraging Fundamentals

Overseas revenues came in at approximately RMB 2 billion in fiscal 2025 (71% growth), with a RMB 3 billion estimate being projected for fiscal 2026 (33% projected growth rate). Furthermore, with the Mainland China business expected to decline by a mid to high teen percentage, these projections comprise an approximate 2% top-line decline in total, which is key for the following reason.

MOMO's average annual top-line decline is closer to the 9% mark over the past five years. By finally being able to stabilize its revenue growth profile and by keeping margins elevated through the transition, you feel that the company now has a clear beeline to make sustained bottom-line growth become a reality. The COO made this clear in the Q4 earnings report with respect to the company's success in overseas markets.

Our overseas business delivered exceptional results last year, fueled by organic product incubation and targeted M&A. This allowed us to diversify our portfolio and rapidly expand our global presence, leading to accelerated revenue momentum. The overseas business is now a solidified revenue contributor and a key engine for our future growth

Confidence Demonstrated Through Continued Special Dividend Payment

Management relayed info in the Q4 earnings report concerning the approval of the special dividend of USD $0.28 per ADS. This means the stock is trading with a yield of approximately 4.47%, which starts to make sense from the long side for the following reason.

Given recent trends, MOMO's EPS revisions have improved significantly, with the current fiscal-2026 estimate of $0.94 per share only losing 1.5%+ of its value over the past nine days. The above estimate equates to approximately 10% bottom-line growth in fiscal 2026, but to remain conservative, we will drop the growth rate to 7% in our modelling, which comes in at approximately $0.91 per share this year.

Therefore, we can state that an investor's rate of return = G + D/M

where D denotes the company's GAAP dividend payout ratio, and M is the GAAP P/E multiple. Therefore, while working off a 7% forward-looking bottom-line growth rate projection in fiscal 2026, we can state that

Rate of Return = 7% + 43%/9.21 = 11.66%.

Suffice it to say, we haven't seen these types of growth projections in quite a while in MOMO, which points to that GAAP figure of RMB 804 million in fiscal 2025, likely being the bottom for this stock in terms of profitability.

Overseas-related growth Remains The Key

As mentioned, overseas revenue streams are responsible for the likely bottom, where strong potential across multiple markets remains in its early innings, as the COO outlined below.

In terms of mix, SoulChill is still the heavy hitter. It brings in over half of our international revenue, but the real growth engine right now is our new MENA products, Yaha Live and Amar. They are scaling fast, and they will be the main drivers for us as we head into 2026. But it's not only just about the Middle East, though; our other markets are also picking up high speed. We have got Tantan International in Asia, MiraiMind in Japan and Happn, which started in Europe and is now moving into Turkey and South America

Conclusion

To sum up, although we require confirmation from the technical chart in due course, MOMO's Q4 and full year trends were encouraging from the standpoint of fresh users on the MOMO app, coupled with robust international expansion. We may upgrade this stock to a Buy in due course, which we will update as a pinned comment under this piece. We look forward to continued coverage

data.title: Compass Therapeutics: Potential Here, But Patience Required
data.description:
Shares of antibody-based oncology concern Compass Therapeutics, Inc. (CMPX), have rallied strongly since data from its lead asset, tovecimig, treating biliary tract cancer [BTC], received a cool reception. Even though it (in combination with chemo) only produced a 17% ORR in a second-line setting, that was three times more than chemo alone, easily making it the standard of care if approved. With PD-1 x PD-L1 bispecific CTX-8371 delivering encouraging early results in an ongoing Phase 1 trial and a $1 billion-plus opportunity for tovecimig treating BTC, Compass merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Compass Therapeutics, Inc. is a Boston-based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development of antibody-based therapies for the treatment of cancers. The company is currently advancing three programs through the clinic, including lead asset tovecimig against multiple solid tumor indications. Compass was formed in 2014 and technically went public in March 2021 after reverse merging into shell company and exchange-orphaned Olivia Ventures in 2020. The company’s shares didn’t trade meaningfully until they listed on the NASDAQ in November 2021, with their first trade conducted at $3.21. Compass’ stock trades around $5.25 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $950 million.

Platform

The company leverages its expertise in angiogenesis (new blood vessel development), the immune system, and their role in tumor growth to develop therapies designed to modulate the microvasculature in the tumor microenvironment, disrupt vessel formation, and shrink tumors. With that charge, Compass has advanced three antibody-based therapeutics into the clinic.

Tovecimig. The company’s most advanced candidate is tovecimig, a bispecific antibody targeting Delta-like ligand 4 (DLL4), a ligand of Notch-1, and vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGF-A) that is undergoing evaluation as a second-line (2L) therapy in a Phase 2/3 trial (COMPANION-002) for the treatment of biliary tract cancer [BTC]. It is also expected to be investigated for the treatment of other DLL4+ tumors. By simultaneously blocking VEGF-A and dysregulated Notch signaling pathways, Compass expects tovecimig to shrink solid tumors and induce apoptosis.

Its most promising indication is BTC, which afflicts ~25,000 in the U.S. annually and ~200,000 worldwide, with only 10% of patients eligible for surgery. The balance is initially treated with a chemotherapy combination of gemcitabine and cisplatin plus either Merck’s (MRK) mega-blockbuster PD-1 inhibitor Keytruda (pembrolizumab) or AstraZeneca’s (AZN) PD-L1 blocking antibody Imfinzi (durvalumab). With an objective response rate [ORR] of 27% to 28% in a 1L setting and two-year overall survival [OS] of 25%, there remains a substantial unmet need.

Furthermore, there is no standard of care for the 2L setting, with current ORRs for best supportive between 0% and 5%, representing a domestic opportunity north of $1 billion. That said, there are targeted therapies in a 2L setting, including Jazz Pharmaceuticals’ (JAZZ) bispecific HER2-directed antibody Ziihera (zanidatamab), which was approved in 2024 for HER2-positive BTC in a 2L setting after demonstrating a 52% ORR and a median duration of response of 14.9 months in its pivotal trial. That said, only ~20% of BTCs are amenable to targeted treatment.

In the ongoing, placebo-controlled, 168-patient COMPANION-002 trial, tovecimig plus chemo agent paclitaxel in a 2L setting achieved its primary endpoint, delivering an ORR of 17% versus paclitaxel monotherapy’s 5% (p=0.031). As of a September 2025 cutoff date, the combo demonstrated greater than 20% OS at 18 months of follow-up. With 80% of OS events triggering an analysis of secondary endpoints progression-free survival and OS, Compass expects to read them out in late 1Q26. A BLA filing is then anticipated in 2H26. Tovecimig is also being investigated in combination with chemo as a 1L treatment in a study sponsored by MD Andersen. It has received a Fast Track designation from the FDA.

The candidate was also investigated as a monotherapy in a Phase 2/3 study treating colorectal cancer in a 3L or 4L setting. However, a dismal ORR (2/40, 5%) in the Phase 2 portion did not meet the criteria for advancement into the trial’s next stage. However, it did demonstrate better outcomes against DLL4+ cancers. Given that many solid tumors express DLL4+, the company expects to initiate a Phase 2 basket trial assessing tovecimig against gastric, ovarian, renal, hepatocellular, and colorectal cancers in 1H26. Management’s ultimate and very lofty goal for this trial would be to employ it as a launching pad to replace Roche’s (RHHBY) VEGF-targeting agent and former mega-blockbuster Avastin (bevacizumab), as well as its biosimilars, in all their approved indications.

CTX-471. The company’s second most advanced candidate is CTX-471, an agonistic IgG4 monoclonal antibody that targets co-stimulatory receptor CD137, which is found on activated T-cells and natural killer cells. In a Phase 1b trial evaluating 60 patients across 17 different tumor types in a 2L+ setting, it generated a 27% partial response rate [PR] in melanoma (3/11) and a 25% PR in mesothelioma (1/4), as well as one complete response in small cell lung cancer (1/3). Furthermore, a positive treatment correlation was found between CTX-471 and tumors expressing meaningful levels of neural cell adhesion molecule (NCAM or CD56). To explore this connection further, Compass is initiating a Phase 2 basket study covering seven NCAM+ tumors in 1H26.

CTX-8371. The company’s other clinical asset is CTX-8371, which is a PD-1 x PD-L1 bispecific antibody that is undergoing investigation in a Phase 1 dose-escalation and dose-expansion study treating solid tumor patients who have progressed after PD-1 or PD-L1 therapy. To date, three responses have been observed in the first 15 patients (five cohorts), one each in non-small cell lung cancer, triple-negative breast cancer, and Hodgkin lymphoma. A more thorough readout of the dose-escalation cohorts is anticipated in 1H26, with dose-expansion cohorts (comprising an additional 15 patients) anticipated to produce data near YE26. Given the enormous success of both PD-1 and PD-L1 monoclonal antibodies, results from Compass’ bispecific, which appears to have the added benefit of engaging T cells, should warrant close attention from the market.

The company also anticipates entering a fourth program, PD-1 x VEGF-A bispecific CTX-10726, into the clinic in 1H26.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

To advance its stable of candidates, Compass held cash and marketable securities of just under $210 million. These funding levels provide an operating runway into 2028, according to management. The company burned through $49 million of cash to support all operations in FY2025 and posted a net loss of $66.5 million for the year.

Sell-side analysts are convinced that these funds will be put to productive use, with the community unanimously positive on the company, as can be seen above. On average, they see tovecimig producing its first meaningful revenue ($53 million) in FY27, ramping to $626 million by FY30.

Verdict:

It is a sad commentary on the BTC treatment space that a therapy with a 17% ORR in a 2L setting is expected to achieve over half a billion in sales by 2030. That seemingly tepid efficacy metric was likely the reason shares of CMPX initially sold off 30% to their 52-week intraday low of $1.33 in the trading session subsequent to its readout on April 1, 2025, remaining more or less below $2 over the ensuing six weeks. They have pushed much higher since.

That said, the space for non-targeted therapies, comprising ~80% of the 2L setting, should be tovecimig’s for the taking, if approved. Furthermore, clinical competition is sparse. And given that 1L therapies can’t achieve 30% ORR, there should be plenty of patients for Compass to treat. Add to tovecimig the potential of CTX-8371, even as a Phase 1 candidate, and it’s easy to ascertain why shares of CMPX are up substantially since their April 2025 low. The stock has a current valuation of ~$750 million net of cash.

Given the large amount of uncertainty in the overall market, I am quite cautious around any entity that is not projected to be profitable until FY2029. I would be willing to take an initial covered call holding in CMPX, but the poor liquidity in the options does not allow for that strategy currently.
